# Common bacteria may directly impact feed efficiency

Infection with Lawsonia damages cells responsible for nutrient capture, reducing feed efficiency by 25%, according to new research.

![multiple-antibiotic-resistant-bacteria](https://img.feedstrategy.com/files/base/wattglobalmedia/all/image/2019/09/fs.multiple-antibiotic-resistant-bacteria.png?auto=format%2Ccompress&q=70&w=400)

## Infection withLawsonia damages cells responsible for nutrient capture, slashing feed efficiency, study finds

Reduced feed intake may not be solely responsible for feed efficiency and performance losses experienced by pigs suffering from ileitis, according to new research from Iowa State University.

After 21 days of infection with [*Lawsonia intracellularis*, the bacteria that causes ileitis](https://www.feedstrategy.com/animal-feed-additives/researchers-hone-in-on-best-probiotic-strains-for-swine/), cells within the affected pig’s intestine may look normal, but they are in fact missing some “digestive and absorptive machinery,” said Nicholas Gabler, one of the study authors and a professor of swine nutrition and physiology at Iowa State University. In particular, the cells appear to have a limited ability to produce an enzyme critical to the digestion of carbohydrates, according to Fernando Leite, technical manager for enteric disease at [Boehringer Ingelheim](/15499626) Animal health, which participated in the study.

While feed intake declined 27% over the course of the infection compared to unaffected pigs, the apparent changes to intestinal cells also contributed to a 25% decrease in feed efficiency, according to [the study](https://veterinaryresearch.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13567-021-00958-2). Vaccination against Lawsonia prior to infection limited the feed efficiency losses to 8%.

“One of my main goals for the last six to seven years has been to walk through different pathogens and help industry understand what is happening, and where are the opportunities to mitigate” the effects of the disease with limited use of antibiotics, Gabler said. In the case of Lawsonia, he said, “we have an understanding of he growth rate difference, because that is easy to collect, but what is really happening with feed efficiency and nutrition in the pig?”

In many of the studies, Gabler said, his work has determined that most of the performance losses are the result of the animal going off its feed. But Lawsonia, it turns out, is an exception.

The study’s results, Gabler said, point to the need for biosecurity to prevent infection. But given the prevalence of Lawsonia — which is estimated to afflict some 30% of pigs at some point during the growing and finishing stages — Gabler said the study also confirmed vaccination can help mitigate the worst effects of the disease.

“Some producers may choose to medicate for this disease, but this study shows this vaccine can be an effective tool to improve feed efficiency and prevent the disease in the first place.” Leite said.

What remains unclear, Gabler said, is exactly how long the damage to the intestinal cells persists after infection with Lawsonia. This particular study did not follow the course of infection after 21 days. So while Gabler doesn’t believe the effects on the intestine are permanent, he said more research is necessary to determine how far beyond 21 days it may persist.

# The effects of mortality on pig feed efficiency

Author: [Dr. Henrique Cemin](/about/senior-leadership/dr-henrique-cemin)

![Pigs in pin](https://www.alltech.com/sites/default/files/styles/16_9_large/public/2023-06/Web%20JPG-Iowa-Pig-108.png.jpg?itok=ulSF0XJq)

*(Editor's note: This is part two of a three-part series on feed efficiency in pigs.)*

Feed efficiency refers to the amount of feed consumed by a pig to produce a unit of weight gain, and it can be influenced by multiple factors, including genetics, nutrition, environment and health. A critical metric for pork producers, feed efficiency has a major impact on the profitability of a pig production system.

### How does mortality affect feed efficiency?

One of the factors that can significantly affect feed efficiency is mortality. Pig mortality is one of the main challenges the pig industry faces today. It is a complex, multifactorial issue that affects most operations and can greatly affect the overall profitability of an operation. Mortality rates are widely dependent on each individual system but typically range 5–10% within wean-to-finish.

**The effects of mortality on pig feed efficiency depend on several factors:**

**1. The main direct impact is a reduction in the number of pigs marketed, leading to an increased cost of production.**

Obviously, when a pig dies before it reaches market weight, the revenue from that animal is completely lost and the cost of producing the pig up to that point cannot be recovered. It is clear, therefore, that mortality events in heavier pigs that were close to marketing have a significantly higher economic impact, as more resources were used and wasted. Additionally, the producer must spend additional funds to properly dispose of the dead animals. The revenue loss and added costs reduce the profitability of the operation.

**2. Mortality can also have an indirect effect by affecting the pen’s social structure.**

When one or more pigs dies, changes in the social structure of the group can lead to stress and increased aggression among the remaining pigs. This can lead to decreased feed intake, reduced growth rates, and increased susceptibility to disease.

**3. Finally, mortality events can alter the performance and feed budgets, and consequently the feed costs, of the remaining pigs.**

If a significant number of pigs die, the amount of feed available is now divided by a smaller number of animals. This means that more feed is provided, per pig, for a longer period. This can have both pros and cons. Typically, less-nutrient-dense feeds are provided as pigs get heavier, to account for changes in nutrient requirements and feed intake. Yet when extra feed is available due to mortality, the remaining pigs receive the more nutrient-dense diets for longer. This will typically improve feed efficiency, but feed cost per pig will be increased, which has a direct impact on the economic viability of the operation.

### Strategies to tackle pig mortality

Fortunately, some strategies can be used to tackle this issue and promote health in the pork industry:

**1. Biosecurity**

Improving biosecurity practices is probably the most important aspect of preventing the introduction and spread of diseases in pig herds. These practices include controlled access to barns; cleaning and disinfection protocols; shower-in and shower-out protocols; and adequate quarantine procedures.

**2. Vaccination**

The implementation of a robust vaccination program that is tailored to the needs of a particular pig herd is also essential. Several options are available to prevent diseases such as porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome (PRRS), porcine circovirus, and swine influenza. By following appropriate guidelines provided by veterinarians, producers can significantly reduce the mortality associated with preventable diseases.

**3. Management**

Management practices can have an important impact on pig health. Staff should be trained to conduct regular monitoring of health indicators, such as body condition and fecal quality, to detect early signs of disease. Hygiene practices, ventilation and adequate floor space play important roles in minimizing stress and disease spread. Additionally, it is critical to ensure that all pigs receive adequate nutrition and unlimited access to a clean and easily accessible water supply.

As always, treatment and prevention options are increasing as science advances. Researchers are currently studying the possibilities of genetic selection in producing pigs more resilient to certain diseases. This could be an invaluable tool to reduce mortality rates in the future.

In summary, mortality has profound direct and indirect impacts on feed efficiency and, therefore, on the profitability of any pork production system. Mortality is certainly a complex issue that requires concerted, multidisciplinary efforts, but it must be addressed for a more sustainable and profitable pork industry.

# What Factors Affect Feed Efficiency in Livestock?

Feed efficiency in livestock is a crucial metric in the agriculture industry, playing a pivotal role in determining the profitability and sustainability of farming operations. As global demand for animal products continues to grow, optimizing how efficiently animals convert feed into meat, milk, or eggs becomes increasingly important. Several interrelated factors influence feed efficiency, each contributing to the overall effectiveness and cost-efficiency of animal production systems.

Firstly, genetic makeup is an inherent determinant that affects how well ani

imals utilize their feed. Breeding for traits that enhance feed conversion ratio (FCR) can lead to significant improvements in livestock productivity. Additionally, the diet’s nutritional composition is vital; it must be carefully balanced to meet the specific needs of the livestock at various stages of their growth and reproduction.

Environmental conditions also play a substantial role. Temperature, humidity, and housing conditions affect the animals’ metabolism and stress levels, which in turn affect their feed intake and utilization. Moreover, health management practices, including vaccination and parasite control, ensure that livestock can utilize their feed optimally by preventing illnesses that can lead to poor feed conversion.

Collectively, these factors demonstrate the complexity of achieving high feed efficiency in livestock. Addressing them effectively requires a holistic approach that incorporates advances in genetics, nutrition science, and animal husbandry. By understanding and optimizing these elements, farmers can improve their production efficiency, reduce costs, and minimize their environmental footprint, aligning with both economic and ecological goals.

![Barn World Banner](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img/https://www.barnworld.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/Barn-World-Banner-2-1-1.png)
![Barn World Banner](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%20210%20140%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

## Genetic Selection

Genetic selection plays a pivotal role in enhancing feed efficiency in livestock, representing the deliberate choice of breeding animals based on specific traits that promote better conversion of feed into body mass. This process directly impacts productivity, profitability, and sustainability in livestock farming. By selecting and breeding individuals with genetically superior traits for feed efficiency, farmers can achieve significant improvements in how animals utilize the nutrients provided in their diets, leading to reduced feed costs and less environmental impact per unit of livestock product produced.

Feed efficiency in livestock is influenced by a range of factors, with genetics being a significant component. Animals that are genetically predisposed to convert feed more efficiently tend to grow faster and use less feed to reach market weight, thus improving economic outcomes for farmers. However, realizing these benefits demands a comprehensive understanding of genetic markers and traits associated with high feed efficiency.

Moreover, the improvement of feed efficiency through genetic selection involves the integration of several other factors including diet composition, health practices, and environmental management. Optimizing diet formulation based on the genetic makeup of livestock can further amplify gains in feed efficiency. That is, livestock bred for specific traits may perform optimally when provided with tailored diets that meet their unique genetic potential.

Health and disease management also plays a crucial role in ensuring that genetically selected animals express their potential for feed efficiency. An animal’s health status can significantly affect its ability to utilize nutrients effectively. Illnesses or parasitic infections can impair digestive efficiency and increase the metabolic energy required to fight off diseases, thereby reducing overall feed efficiency.

Environmental conditions are another critical factor influencing the effectiveness of genetic selection for improved feed efficiency. Factors such as temperature, housing, and stocking density can all impact an animal’s stress levels and metabolic rates, which in turn affect feed intake and utilization. Thus, creating an environment that minimizes stress and maximizes comfort is essential for allowing genetically superior animals to express their potential fully.

In conclusion, while genetic selection is a powerful tool in improving feed efficiency in livestock, its success is interdependent with optimal management in diet, health, and environmental conditions. By addressing these factors holistically, livestock producers can maximize the benefits of genetic advancements and sustainably enhance their operations.

## Diet Composition

Diet composition is crucial for optimizing feed efficiency in livestock. This refers to the proportions and types of nutrients included in the diets of farm animals. An ideal diet should provide all the necessary nutrients required for the animals’ growth, reproduction, and health. The main components of livestock diets usually include carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, and minerals. Each of these components plays a vital role in various physiological processes of the animal.

Carbohydrates are a major source of energy in most livestock diets, particularly those that are plant-based. They are important for maintaining animal activity and supporting metabolic processes. Proteins are essential for the growth and repair of body tissues and are particularly critical for young, growing animals as well as lactating females. Fats, while more energy-dense than carbohydrates, provide essential fatty acids and aid in the absorption of fat-soluble vitamins.

The balance and formulation of these nutrients can significantly impact feed efficiency, which measures the animal’s ability to convert feed into desired outputs like meat, milk, or eggs. Highly digestible ingredients tend to improve feed efficiency because they allow animals to absorb more nutrients from less feed. Therefore, the choice and quality of raw materials in animal feeds are pivotal. Feed ingredients should be selected based on their nutrient availability, digestibility, and cost-effectiveness.

Other dietary factors such as the inclusion of additives like enzymes, probiotics, and prebiotics can enhance nutrient absorption and digestion, further optimizing feed efficiency. The physical form of the feed (pelleted vs. mash) also influences feed intake and digestibility.

Feed efficiency in livestock is influenced by several factors:

1. \*\*Genetics\*\*: Animals genetically predisposed to better digestiveness or quicker growth can utilize feed more efficiently.  
 2. \*\*Health\*\*: Diseases and parasites can severely reduce an animal’s ability to process and convert feed efficiently.  
 3. \*\*Environmental conditions\*\*: Extreme temperatures or inadequate housing can increase stress in animals, leading to poor feed conversion.  
 4. \*\*Feeding management\*\*: How, when, and where feed is delivered can influence consumption and wastage levels, impacting overall feed efficiency.

By understanding and optimizing these factors, livestock producers can achieve better growth rates and productivity from their animals, reducing waste and improving profitability. Understanding the interplay between diet composition and these various factors is key to successful livestock management.

## Health and Disease Management

Health and disease management is an essential factor that significantly affects livestock productivity, including feed efficiency. This field encompasses a variety of practices aimed at preventing diseases and maintaining optimal health, which directly influences the animal’s ability to utilize feed effectively and grow.

Effective health and disease management in livestock includes regular health checks, vaccinations, parasite control, and prompt treatment of illnesses. Vaccinations help prevent a range of infectious diseases that can cause reduced feed intake, poor nutrient absorption, and the diversion of nutrients from growth to immune response. Similarly, controlling parasites is critical because parasitic infections can lead to poor feed conversion, weight loss, and general poor health.

Another aspect of health management is the maintenance of a stress-free environment, as stress can lead to increased susceptibility to diseases and inefficient feed utilization. Practices such as proper handling, adequate housing, and minimizing transportation can alleviate stress and improve overall health status, thus promoting better feed efficiency.

Feed efficiency in livestock is also influenced by multiple interrelated factors apart from health and disease management. These factors include genetic selection, which determines the inherent potential of an animal to convert feed into body mass. Different breeds have varying genetic potential for feed efficiency, and selective breeding can enhance desirable traits.

Diet composition significantly impacts feed efficiency as well. Diets must be balanced in nutrients and matched to the specific growth stages and health needs of the animals. The inclusion of high-quality and digestible ingredients ensures that livestock can derive maximum energy and nutrients from their feed, potentially reducing the quantity of feed needed for maintenance and growth.

Environmental conditions are another important factor. Extreme temperatures, humidity, and poor ventilation can stress animals and lead to inefficient feed conversion. Proper management of the environment, ensuring comfortable housing and climatic conditions, helps livestock maintain healthy appetites and efficient metabolism.

Lastly, feeding management practices such as the timing of feed delivery, the consistency of the diet, and the physical form of the feed can all affect how well feed is utilized. Managing these factors ensures that livestock can eat their fill during optimal times, aiding in better digestion and nutrient uptake.

In conclusion, optimizing each of these factors can lead to significant improvements in feed efficiency in livestock, which is crucial for the economic sustainability of farming operations and the environmental impact of livestock production.

## Environmental Conditions

Environmental conditions play a significant role in the overall feed efficiency of livestock. These conditions include but are not limited to temperature, humidity, air quality, and housing. Each of these factors can influence the metabolic rate of animals and affect their growth, productivity, and health, which in turn impacts their efficiency in converting feed into body mass.

Temperature is one of the critical environmental factors affecting feed efficiency. Extremely high or low temperatures can cause stress in animals, leading them to expend energy maintaining their body temperature rather than growing or producing milk or eggs. This stress response can increase the maintenance energy requirements and reduce feed efficiency. For instance, cattle are known to consume less feed in hot weather conditions, yet their maintenance energy requirements increase due to the additional energy expended for cooling their bodies.

Humidity interacts with temperature to exacerbate or mitigate heat stress. High humidity levels can make high temperatures even more detrimental to livestock by reducing the effectiveness of natural cooling processes like sweating and panting. Livestock unable to effectively cool themselves may suffer from heat stress which leads to decreased appetite and poor nutrient utilization.

Air quality, including the concentration of harmful gases such as ammonia and dust levels, directly affects the health of the respiratory system of livestock. Poor air quality can lead to respiratory diseases, which can impair feed intake and nutrient absorption, thus reducing feed efficiency.

Proper housing is also essential for ensuring optimal environmental conditions. Adequate shelter not only provides protection from extreme weather conditions, but also helps in managing cleanliness, reducing disease risk, and maintaining an environment conducive to effective resting and feeding behaviors.

To enhance feed efficiency under varying environmental conditions, farmers and ranchers can adopt various management practices. These include implementing climate-controlled housing, ensuring good ventilation, providing adequate shade and water during hot weather, and utilizing breeds that are better adapted to specific climates or environmental stresses.

Feed efficiency in livestock can also be influenced by a multitude of other factors including genetic selection, diet, health, and feeding management practices. Each of these factors interacts complexly with environmental conditions, influencing the overall nutritional status and productive efficiency of the livestock. Efficient feed use in livestock farming not only reduces the cost of production but also minimizes the environmental impact of farming practices by decreasing waste and emissions. Therefore, understanding and managing environmental factors is crucial for sustainable livestock management and optimizing feed efficiency.

## Feeding Management Practices

Feeding management practices are crucial for optimizing feed efficiency in livestock. These practices encompass several considerations and actions taken to ensure that animals are not only fed adequate quantities but also in ways that promote better digestion and nutrient absorption. Effective feeding management can significantly influence the health, productivity, and overall performance of the livestock.

One of the core aspects of feeding management is the feeding schedule. Regular and consistent feeding times help regulate the animals’ digestion processes and nutrient uptake. It also helps in avoiding digestive problems, which can arise from irregular feeding times. Additionally, the physical form of the feed, whether it is pelleted, mashed, or in roughage form, plays a significant role in how well animals can ingest and digest their food. For instance, finer particles are typically easier for animals to digest compared to larger, coarser particles.

Another crucial aspect is the precision in dietary formulation. This involves calculating the optimal nutrient ratios required by different classes of livestock based on their stage of growth, production demands, or reproductive status. Precision feeding helps in reducing waste of nutrients and ensures that animals are not over or underfed, both of, which can negatively impact feed efficiency.

Portion control is also integral to feeding management, ensuring that each animal receives a portion that is tailored to its specific nutritional needs. Overfeeding can lead to fat accumulation which is inefficient in terms of feed conversion, whereas underfeeding can result in inadequate growth or production. Moreover, adopting technologies like automated feeding systems can enhance feeding accuracy and consistency, which in turn can optimize feed intake and digestion.

Environmental conditions surrounding feeding areas also influence how effectively animals can consume their feed. For example, a calm, comfortable feeding environment reduces stress among livestock which can otherwise negatively affect their ability to digest and utilize nutrients effectively.

In conclusion, comprehensive and thoughtful feeding management practices are essential for enhancing feed efficiency in livestock. These practices contribute to better health, optimal growth, and higher productivity, which are vital for the economic success of farming operations. By focusing on the schedule, feed form, dietary precision, and suitable feeding environments, farmers can substantially improve the efficiency of feed utilization in their livestock production operations.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Infections like Lawsonia significantly impair nutrient absorption, leading to a 25% reduction in feed efficiency, highlighting the need for effective disease management strategies.
Extreme temperatures and humidity can elevate metabolic stress, reducing feed intake and efficiency, thus necessitating better environmental management practices for optimal livestock health.
Integrating biosecurity, vaccination, and health monitoring is essential to mitigate disease impacts and optimize feed efficiency, ensuring sustainable livestock production and profitability.
